originalTitle: How Aldi is taking on US supermarkets with its $4 almond butter
summary: The German supermarket's $9bn US push targets urban hubs like Manhattan. Can its discount model match Walmart?
content:
Mary Porter shops at Aldi's new Manhattan location in New York City for the first time

When Mary Porter walked into Manhattan's newest Aldi store hunting for bargains, the long-time resident found what she considered a retail miracle in plain sight: a $4 jar of almond butter that costs $22 in her own neighbourhood.

"Aldi has the reputation for being inexpensive, so I thought I would come and check it out, and by golly, it is amazing," Porter, 79, told the BBC, marvelling at the savings alongside the fresh spinach and organic raspberries filling her basket.

To the unassuming passer-by, the storefront is completely hidden, tucked away in an underground car park beneath The Ellery, a luxury apartment complex where the cheapest rent starts at nearly $5,000 (£3,725) a month.

In fact, the building's own website completely omits the grocer from its curated online neighbourhood guide, choosing instead to highlight pricier nearby options like Whole Foods and Brooklyn Fare.

But step past the luxury façade into the basement, and the quiet disappears. Even on an early Tuesday afternoon in July, the brightly lit, bustling space hums with high energy as a lunchtime crowd of New Yorkers tightly navigates the narrow aisles with oversized canvas bags.

Porter's discovery is part of Aldi's $9bn US expansion plan to add 800 new stores over five years, specifically targeting dense urban hubs like Manhattan. It marks a massive scale-up for the German supermarket, which first entered the US in 1976 and has steadily grown its footprint to nearly 2,800 storefronts.

The aggressive real estate blitz signals a bold shift for a brand traditionally associated with suburban strip malls and lower-end consumers.

The Manhattan location is one of 800 new store openings Aldi has planned over five years

Incumbent US grocers may look with some concern at the insurgency Aldi pulled off since it entered the UK market in the 1990s.

Alongside fellow German supermarket Lidl, Aldi picked up huge swathes of the market by offering cheaper prices for high-quality goods. The traditional "big four" grocers at the time - Tesco, Sainsbury's, Asda and Morrisons - were slow to respond to the new competition, leaving the challengers to gradually pick off their shoppers.

Today, Aldi is the UK's fourth biggest grocer, commanding 10.8% of the market.

Its rapid growth is being mirrored across Europe, its rise aided by easing perceptions of it as a strictly lower cost grocer as shoppers became increasingly impressed by the quality of its products. The cost of living crisis of the 2020s further fuelled its ascent.

However, while Aldi is rapidly ascending the ranks of American grocery consciousness, it is not, and may never aim to be, Walmart.

Aldi currently holds just 2.9% of the US grocery pie, while Walmart controls about 20%.

Yet analysts say that staying smaller is precisely how Aldi wins.

Data from location analytics firm Placer.ai reveals Aldi is capturing middle- and higher-income shoppers with household incomes between $75,000 and $125,000.

Years of persistent inflation have forced wealthier households to aggressively seek cheaper supermarkets.

"Those shoppers have started to trade off a visit to a conventional grocery store or a quick service restaurant and started to go into Aldi more frequently," R.J. Hottovy, Placer.ai's head of analytical research, told the BBC. "They're looking for ways to stretch their household budget."

Walmart warns US shoppers are cutting spending as higher petrol prices bite

For some urban commuters, the new city location offers a better experience than older formats. Kelvin Dozier, who usually shops at an Aldi in Brooklyn, recently started visiting the Manhattan location right across from his office for convenience.

"The one here - it's brighter," Dozier told the BBC, noting the fresh sweet navel oranges in his basket. "The one in Brooklyn is a little smaller. It almost seems temporary, but here it looks like a permanent location."

Still, winning over city slickers accustomed to premium brands remains an uphill battle. Ralph Montenegro, visiting Aldi for the first time, remained fiercely loyal to competitors.

"It has more variety than say Target," Montenegro said, praising the prices on staples like flour and fruit, though he noted he still prefers Trader Joe's. He added that Aldi's heavy reliance on packaged, private-label processed foods was a detractor compared to the natural organic options he prefers.

This strict reliance on limited, private labels is exactly what keeps Aldi's overheads low, according to Dustin York, an associate professor of communication at Maryville University.

He says that Aldi targets a lean, highly efficient model that provides about 80% of what a traditional big-box retailer carries, but at a much lower cost.

Still, York argues it is unlikely that Aldi will take dramatic market share from Walmart, because the retail giant is simply too massive. "I call Walmart the battleship, and I call Aldi a kind of submarine."

But navigating those crowded waters can bring a distinct financial hazard.

"Their biggest kryptonite is real estate cost," York warned, pointing to a brutal Manhattan retail landscape where average asking rents are between $350 and $700 per square foot.

Aldi hopes it can grow its share of the US grocery market in the same way it has in Europe

In addition to high rents, Manhattan's roads provide another challenge.

Speaking on Bloomberg's Odd Lots podcast, Aldi's US chief commercial officer Scott Patton detailed that supplying the Manhattan store requires trucking inventory in from South Windsor, Connecticut, using shorter, specialised trucks to navigate tight city streets.

"We come at night because of the congestion," Patton said, noting that each truck requires a two-driver team to handle the city's turning radiuses. One driver watches for blind spots while the other unloads the groceries. To keep shelves in the Manhattan location stocked, A
chineseTitle: Aldi 如何用 4 美元的杏仁黄油挑战美国超市
chineseSummary: 这家德国超市在美国投入 90 亿美元，目标是曼哈顿等城市中心。其折扣模式能与沃尔玛媲美吗？
chineseContent:
玛丽·波特 (Mary Porter) 首次在阿尔迪 (Aldi) 纽约曼哈顿新店购物

当玛丽·波特 (Mary Porter) 走进曼哈顿最新的阿尔迪 (Aldi) 商店寻找便宜货时，这位长期居民发现了她眼中的零售奇迹：一罐 4 美元的杏仁黄油在她自己的社区售价 22 美元。

79 岁的波特对 BBC 表示：“阿尔迪以物美价廉着称，所以我想来看看，天哪，这真是太棒了。”她对篮子里装满的新鲜菠菜和有机覆盆子的省钱感到惊讶。

对于不起眼的路人来说，店面是完全隐藏的，隐藏在豪华公寓大楼 The Ellery 下方的地下停车场中，最便宜的租金为每月近 5,000 美元（3,725 英镑）。

事实上，该建筑自己的网站在其精心策划的在线社区指南中完全省略了杂货店，而是选择突出显示附近价格较高的选择，例如 Whole Foods 和 Brooklyn Fare。

但越过豪华的外观进入地下室，安静就消失了。即使在七月的一个周二下午早些时候，这个灯火通明、熙熙攘攘的空间也充满了活力，午餐时间的纽约人带着超大帆布袋紧紧地穿过狭窄的过道。

波特的发现是阿尔迪在美国 90 亿美元扩张计划的一部分，该计划将在五年内新增 800 家新店，特别针对曼哈顿等密集的城市中心。这标志着这家德国超市的大规模扩张，该超市于 1976 年首次进入美国，目前已拥有近 2,800 个店面。

激进的房地产闪电战标志着这个传统上与郊区购物中心和低端消费者联系在一起的品牌的大胆转变。

曼哈顿店是阿尔迪五年内计划开设的 800 家新店之一

阿尔迪 (Aldi) 自 20 世纪 90 年代进入英国市场以来所掀起的叛乱可能令美国现有杂货商有些担忧。

阿尔迪与德国超市利德尔 (Lidl) 一起，通过提供更便宜的高品质商品赢得了巨大的市场份额。当时的传统“四大”杂货商——特易购、塞恩斯伯里、阿斯达和莫里森——对新的竞争反应迟缓，让挑战者逐渐抢走他们的顾客。

如今，阿尔迪是英国第四大杂货商，占据 10.8% 的市场份额。

它的快速增长正在整个欧洲得到反映，随着消费者对其产品质量的印象越来越深刻，人们不再认为它是一家严格低成本的杂货商，这也有助于它的崛起。 2020年代的生活成本危机进一步推动了其上涨。

然而，虽然阿尔迪正在迅速提升美国食品杂货意识的排名，但它不是，也可能永远不会成为沃尔玛。

阿尔迪目前仅持有美国食品杂货市场 2.9% 的份额，而沃尔玛则控制着约 20% 的份额。

然而分析人士表示，保持规模较小正是阿尔迪的获胜之道。

位置分析公司 Placer.ai 的数据显示，Aldi 正在吸引家庭收入在 75,000 美元至 125,000 美元之间的中高收入购物者。

多年持续的通货膨胀迫使富裕家庭积极寻找更便宜的超市。

“这些购物者已经开始放弃去传统杂货店或快餐店，而开始更频繁地去阿尔迪，”R.J. Placer.ai 分析研究主管 Hottovy 告诉 BBC。 “他们正在寻找扩大家庭预算的方法。”

沃尔玛警告美国消费者因汽油价格上涨而削减支出

对于一些城市通勤者来说，新的城市位置比旧格式提供了更好的体验。凯尔文·多齐尔（Kelvin Dozier）通常在布鲁克林的阿尔迪（Aldi）购物，最近为了方便起见，他开始前往位于他办公室对面的曼哈顿店。

“这里的那个 - 它更明亮，”多齐尔告诉 BBC，他注意到篮子里有新鲜的甜脐橙。 “布鲁克林的那个要小一些。它看起来几乎是临时的，但在这里它看起来像是一个永久的地点。”

尽管如此，赢得习惯于高端品牌的都市人的青睐仍然是一场艰苦的战斗。拉尔夫·蒙特内格罗（Ralph Montenegro）首次访问阿尔迪，仍然对竞争对手保持着强烈的忠诚。

Montenegro 说：“它的品种比 Target 还要多。”他对面粉和水果等主食的价格表示赞赏，但他指出自己仍然更喜欢 Trader Joe's。他补充说，与他喜欢的天然有机食品相比，阿尔迪对包装、自有品牌加工食品的严重依赖是一个不利因素。

玛丽维尔大学传播学副教授达斯汀·约克表示，这种对有限的自有品牌的严格依赖正是阿尔迪保持较低管理费用的原因。

他表示，阿尔迪的目标是建立一种精益、高效的模式，提供传统大型零售商约 80% 的商品，但成本却低得多。

不过，约克认为阿尔迪不太可能从沃尔玛手中夺取巨大的市场份额，因为这家零售巨头规模太大了。 “我称沃尔玛为战舰，而我称阿尔迪为潜艇。”

但在这些拥挤的水域航行可能会带来明显的财务风险。

约克警告说：“他们最大的克星就是房地产成本。”他指出，曼哈顿的零售业环境十分残酷，平均租金要价在每平方英尺 350 美元到 700 美元之间。

阿尔迪希望能够像在欧洲一样扩大其在美国食品杂货市场的份额

除了高昂的租金之外，曼哈顿的道路也带来了另一个挑战。

阿尔迪美国首席商务官斯科特·巴顿 (Scott Patton) 在彭博社的 Odd Lot 播客上发表讲话时详细说明，为曼哈顿商店供货需要使用较短的专用卡车从康涅狄格州南温莎运来库存，并使用较短的专用卡车在狭窄的城市街道上行驶。

巴顿说：“由于交通拥堵，我们在晚上来。”他指出，每辆卡车都需要两名司机的团队来处理城市的转弯半径。一名司机留意盲点，另一名司机则负责卸货。为了保持曼哈顿分店的货架库存充足，A
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
